tested_predictions.hypothesis: XGBoost will outperform Logistic Regression by ≥4pp macro_f1 because Total_EMI_per_month has mutual_information=0.5471 but F_statistic=1.04, indicating strong nonlinear signal that LR cannot capture

Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP with mean |SHAP| ≥1.3× the SHAP value of Monthly_Inhand_Salary because its MI=0.5471 slightly exceeds Monthly_Inhand_Salary’s MI=0.5187 and trees exploit its nonlinearity
tested_predictions.basis: MI Total_EMI_per_month=0.5471 vs Monthly_Inhand_Salary=0.5187 and F Total_EMI_per_month=1.04 vs F Monthly_Inhand_Salary=1903.07

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Monthly_Inhand_Salary will occupy a top-2 position by absolute coefficient magnitude in Logistic Regression because it has the highest F_statistic=1903.07 and MI=0.5187 among numeric features

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Standard will have the lowest recall (≤0.75) because its class_means on Monthly_Inhand_Salary (4242 between Good 5389 and Poor 3375) and Delay_from_due_date (19.75 between 10.89 and 29.74) create two confusion boundaries
supported_conjectures.basis: class_means overlap for Standard in Salary and Delay_from_due_date
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: per-class recall and confusion matrix at evaluate-models

supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: SHAP feature rank lists for both models in global-xai

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The ratio Total_EMI_per_month/Monthly_Inhand_Salary may achieve MI>0.60 and boost macro_f1 by ≥1pp because EMI shows high nonlinear signal (MI=0.5471, F=1.04) and Salary shows strong linear signal (MI=0.5187, F=1903.07), so the ratio captures residual patterns
exploratory_leads.basis: disagreement in MI vs F for EMI and high MI+F for Salary
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: engineer the ratio feature, retrain models, and compare macro_f1 and SHAP ranking

eda_hypotheses.model_selection_prediction: XGBoost will achieve macro_f1 ≥0.76, outperforming Logistic Regression by ≥4pp because the high MI nonlinear signal in Total_EMI_per_month (0.5471 MI vs F=1.04) and uncorrelated features favor tree-based splits
eda_hypotheses.class_struggle_prediction: Standard will have the lowest recall (≤0.75) with Standard→Good as the dominant confusion due to its intermediate means on Delay_from_due_date and Monthly_Inhand_Salary creating a tighter boundary with Good

Good.diagnosis: High recall (0.817) on XGBoost. Of its 677 errors, 575 go to Standard vs 102 to Poor (5.6×). Feature space clearly separates Good from Poor but overlaps with Standard along intermediate values, so misclassifications are almost entirely Good→Standard.

Poor.diagnosis: Moderate recall (0.769). Of its 1 322 errors, 770 go to Standard vs 552 to Good (1.4×). Poor and Standard share feature overlap, especially on middle-range income and delay values, pulling Poor samples toward Standard more often.

Standard.diagnosis: Lowest recall (0.598). Of its 4 252 errors, 2 220 go to Poor vs 2 032 to Good (1.09×). The Standard class sits between Good and Poor in key features, creating two thin boundaries; errors are nearly evenly split but slightly favor Standard→Poor.

capacity_analysis.logistic_regression: Underfitting. Best C=0.0019 implies heavy L2 regularisation; test macro_F1=0.637 vs CV=0.625 (1.2 pp gap) shows no overfitting but inability to capture nonlinear boundaries. 4.4 pp deficit vs XGBoost confirms limited linear capacity.
capacity_analysis.random_forest: Well-fitted. Best max_depth=10, min_samples_split=4; test macro_F1=0.679 vs CV=0.671 (0.8 pp gap) indicates low variance, sufficient depth to capture interactions without over-complexity.
capacity_analysis.xgboost: Well-fitted high-capacity. max_depth=6, learning_rate≈0.014, subsample/colsample≈0.8. Validation loss declines steadily through 1 000+ rounds with no divergence. Test macro_F1=0.683 vs CV=0.671 (1.2 pp gap) shows slight variance but no overfitting.

training_diagnostics.confidence_analysis: LR shows almost no self-knowledge (correct vs wrong mean conf: 0.6316 vs 0.6270, gap 0.005). RF has modest competence (0.6916 vs 0.6420, gap 0.050). XGBoost has clear confidence separation (0.7511 vs 0.6549, gap 0.096), making its probabilities useful for downstream thresholding.
training_diagnostics.learning_curve_interpretation: XGBoost train and validation mlogloss both decrease monotonically through 1 000 rounds with no sign of divergence, indicating capacity is not yet exhausted and regularisation (subsample/colsample) prevents overfitting. Further rounds may still yield slight gains.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Adjusting the classification threshold for Standard upward (e.g. from 0.33 to 0.40) will raise Standard recall by ≥5pp with ≤1pp drop in overall accuracy
tested_predictions.basis: Standard has low recall (0.598) but XGBoost confidence gap (correct vs wrong ~0.096) suggests threshold tuning can recover false negatives

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Introducing EMI_to_sal_ratio will reduce Standard→Poor confusion by ≥10% in XGBoost
supported_conjectures.basis: High Standard→Poor error (2 220 samples); ratio feature captures residual nonlinear debt burden signal
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Train XGBoost with the ratio feature and compare confusion matrix

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Extending XGBoost to 2 000 rounds will further improve macro_f1 by ≥0.5pp before plateauing
exploratory_leads.basis: Validation mlogloss continues declining at round 1 000 with no flattening, indicating capacity to learn
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Increase n_estimators to 2 000 and monitor macro_f1 and mlogloss

observations: Outstanding_Debt is the top SHAP feature with mean|SHAP|=0.3364, followed by Interest_Rate (0.3111), Month (0.1650), Delay_from_due_date (0.1533) and Credit_Mix_Standard (0.1088).

PDP for Outstanding_Debt shows non-monotonic multi-class effects: Good probability declines steadily above ~500 then plateaus (~0.26), Poor probability rises sharply above ~1000 to ~0.52, while Standard probability falls until ~1500 then rises back above 0.40 by ~3500.

PDP for Interest_Rate is also nonlinear: Good probability peaks ~0.36 at 4%, then drops to ~0.13 by 25–30%; Poor probability climbs from ~0.28 to ~0.51 above 20%; Standard peaks ~0.54 at ~13% then declines.

Representative Good cases (e.g. row 10417) show Num_Credit_Card pushing strongly positive (SHAP=0.7207) in a region (0–2 cards) where PDP places Good probability at ~0.41, indicating model capitalizes on low card counts for Good.

Worst Good→Standard misclassifications (rows 15594, 2008) share high positive Month contributions (SHAP≈0.52–0.59) and Credit_Mix_Standard (≈0.50), mirroring PDP thresholds at Month≥4 that boost Standard probability.

Worst Poor→Standard errors (row 16457) likewise show Month (0.4655) and Credit_Mix_Standard (0.4134) dominating, signaling a common failure mode where months≥4 and standard mix override class distinctions.
insights: The model relies on nonlinear thresholds in Outstanding_Debt and Interest_Rate to distinguish classes, creating complex decision boundaries that a linear model would miss, consistent with capacity analysis favoring XGBoost.

Month acts as a proxy for seasonal or time-on-book effects: values ≥4 dramatically shift probability to Standard, causing misclassifications for both Good and Poor when other signals conflict, revealing an overreliance on temporal features for the middle class.

High SHAP for Num_Credit_Card in representative Good but negative in borderline Good indicates Num_Credit_Card discriminates Good vs Standard along a steep PDP drop at 3 cards, but lacks nuance beyond that threshold, contributing to borderline ambiguity.

feature_importance_consensus.interpretation: PFI results are unavailable; SHAP identifies top nonlinear drivers. Without PFI we cannot assess redundancy vs interaction contributions, but PDP confirms these SHAP drivers exhibit strong marginal and threshold effects.

casebook_analysis.worst_misclassification_pattern: Both Good→Standard and Poor→Standard errors occur when Month≥4 and Credit_Mix_Standard=1, overriding high Outstanding_Debt or Interest_Rate signals and pushing to Standard with >0.90 confidence.
casebook_analysis.borderline_pattern: Borderline Good cases cluster at Num_Credit_Card≈2–3 and Monthly_Balance slightly below average, where SHAP signs flip and PDP curves are steepest, indicating the decision boundary lies at these thresholds.
casebook_analysis.representative_pattern: High-confidence correct predictions for Good and Poor occur at extreme Outstanding_Debt or Interest_Rate values (low debt/low rate for Good; high debt/high rate for Poor) well outside the central overlap region.

cross_layer_validation.eda_to_xai: EDA hypothesized Total_EMI_per_month would dominate; SHAP shows it is absent from the top 15, so the model instead learned debt and rate nonlinearity rather than EMI signals.
cross_layer_validation.fe_impact: Engineered ratios (EMI_to_sal_ratio, inquiries_per_loan, cards_per_account) contribute modestly in local SHAP (e.g. cards_per_account ~0.10) but did not surface globally, suggesting they add incremental but not primary signal.
cross_layer_validation.overfitting_effect: XGBoost shows slight variance but no overfitting; SHAP importance aligns with PDP shapes rather than idiosyncratic noise. The absence of EMI features at top implies model generalizes on core debt/rate patterns rather than memorizing engineered ratios.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Ablating Month will decrease Standard recall by ≥5pp and improve Good/Poor recall by ≥3pp
tested_predictions.basis: Worst misclassifications cluster on Month≥4 with high SHAP contributions for Standard
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Retrain model without Month feature and compare per-class recall changes

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Adding a squared Outstanding_Debt term will reduce Good→Standard errors by ≥10%
supported_conjectures.basis: PDP non-monotonicity and high SHAP for debt indicate nonlinear boundary around 1500
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Train with debt and debt², evaluate Good→Standard confusion rate

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Interactions between Month and Changed_Credit_Limit capture temporal credit policy effects and will enter top-5 SHAP features, reducing Standard misclassifications in months 4–8
exploratory_leads.basis: Local SHAP shows Changed_Credit_Limit and Month jointly drive Standard errors
exploratory_leads.implication: If true, time-dependent credit limit changes are critical and should be incorporated as interaction features
